q: Thermodynamics is the study of systems involving energy in the form of heat and work.
When two systems are in thermal equilibrium, there is no net heat transfer between them.
Systems at the same temperature will be in thermal equilibrium with each other.
The zeroth law of thermodynamics states that when two objects are separately in thermodynamic equilibrium with a third object, they all are in thermodynamic equilibrium with each other.
The first law of thermodynamics is an application of the conservation energy for heat and thermodynamic processes.
The change in internal energy $\Delta U$ of a system is equal to the net heat flow $Q$ into the system minus the total work $W$ done by the system.
$Q$ is positive if heat is added to the system, and negative if heat is removed.
$W$ is positive if work is done by the system, and negative if work is done on the system.
The internal energy $U$ of a system is defined as the sum of the energies of all the constituent particles.
The internal energy of ideal gases depends only on temperature, not on volume or pressure.
The internal energy of an ideal gas is directly proportional to the absolute temperature.
Heat transfer and work are both stored as such in a system.
Internal energy is a form of energy completely different from either heat or work.
A thermodynamic process may be defined as a change from one equilibrium state of a system to another.
In thermodynamics, a quasi-static process is a process that happens slowly enough for the system to remain in internal physical thermodynamic equilibrium.
An isobaric process occurs at constant pressure.
The work done by the system in an isobaric process is simply the pressure multiplied by the change in volume.
In an isobaric process, the work is equal to the area of the shaded rectangle on a pressure Vs volume graph.
An isochoric process occurs at constant volume.
In an isochoric process, no work is done by the system because $W = p \Delta V = 0$.
According to the first law, the change in internal energy must equal any heat flowing into or out of the system in an isochoric process.
$c_v$ is specific heat capacity at constant volume.
$C_V$ is molar heat capacity at constant volume.
For an ideal monatomic gas, the change in internal energy is expressed as $\Delta U = \frac{3}{2} n R \Delta T$.
An isothermal process occurs at a constant temperature (i.e. $\Delta T = 0$).
For a process to be isothermal, any heat flow into or out of the system must occur slowly to maintain thermodynamic equilibrium.
For an ideal gas, internal energy $U$ is only a function of the temperature.
For an isothermal process, $\Delta U$ is equal to zero.
In an isothermal process, $W = Q$.
If the system does work in an isothermal process, the energy comes from heat flowing into the system from the reservoir.
In an adiabatic process, there is no heat transfer into or out of the system, i.e. $Q = 0$.
An adiabatic process can be done by enclosing the system in a thermal insulator (adiabatic container).
For an adiabatic process, the first law of thermodynamics is reduced to $\Delta U = -W$.
$\gamma$ is the ratio of the heat capacities, $\gamma = \frac{C_p}{C_v}$.
The temperature of a gas increases when it is adiabatically compressed.
